category.label: Exponents and Radicals
category.description: Exponent laws, radical notation, rational exponents, and equations involving powers and roots. These skills are the operational core of Unit 2 (exponential and logarithmic functions) and prerequisite for rational-exponent manipulation, complex zeros, and exact trig values.
category.yield_rationale: Exponent laws are the algebraic engine of AP Precalc Unit 2 (2.3, 2.4, 2.9–2.13); rational exponents and radical skills appear in FRQ 4 (symbolic manipulations) and throughout MC; spec anchors integer exponent laws at 0.92.

umbrellas.label: Exponent Notation and Structure
umbrellas.description: Meaning and structure of powers: base/exponent identification, repeated multiplication, how parentheses determine base scope, and coefficient scope errors.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Structural-vocabulary umbrella feeding all downstream use; rarely the direct exam target itself. Rescored from 0.65 to 0.50 to spread the yield signal and better reflect that this umbrella is a prerequisite scaffold, not a direct exam target. Split from original 13-item umbrella; first half covers notation meaning and base-scope errors.

in_depth.label: Base and exponent identification
in_depth.description: A power has a base (the factor being repeated) and an exponent (the count of repetitions); this skill tests reading exponent notation before applying any law.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Entry-level reading skill; foundational but rarely the direct test object; embedded in every exponent problem.

in_depth.label: Positive integer exponents as repeated multiplication
in_depth.description: A positive integer exponent represents repeated multiplication of the same base; excludes zero, negative, and rational exponents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual anchor for exponent meaning; tested at foundation level before laws are applied.

in_depth.label: Power notation with variable bases
in_depth.description: Variable bases written with exponents represent repeated multiplication of the same variable; tests forms like x^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Prerequisite notation for all algebraic exponent work; foundational.

in_depth.label: Power notation with multi-variable bases
in_depth.description: A grouped multi-variable product raised to a power has the entire product as base; distinguishes (xy)^n from xy^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Scope-of-base errors are common in power-of-product problems; moderately tested via application.

in_depth.label: Parenthesized versus unparenthesized bases
in_depth.description: Parentheses determine whether an exponent applies to an entire quantity or only the immediately preceding factor; targets (-x)^2 versus -x^2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign errors from missing parentheses are among the most common algebra mistakes; high distractor value in MC.

in_depth.label: Coefficient not in base without parentheses
in_depth.description: In a term like 4x^2, the exponent applies to x only, not the coefficient 4; distinguishes 4x^2 from (4x)^2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Classic coefficient-scope error targeted in both procedural and error-analysis MC items.

in_depth.label: Powers of grouped sums and differences
in_depth.description: An exponent outside parentheses applies to the entire grouped sum or difference; structural recognition, not binomial expansion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Prerequisite for recognizing when expansion is needed versus when base scope applies; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Powers do not distribute over addition
in_depth.description: A power of a sum is not equal to the sum of powers; targets the misconception (a+b)^n = a^n + b^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: One of the highest-frequency algebra errors; appears as a distractor in polynomial and exponential MC problems.

umbrellas.label: Exponent Notation — Precedence and Edge Cases
umbrellas.description: Exponent precedence over multiplication and addition, subtraction non-distribution, and trivial-base edge cases (power of 1, base of 1).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Precedence and edge-case knowledge is foundational but even lower direct-test frequency than base-scope errors; slightly lower yield than the first notation half. Split from original exponent_notation_and_structure (second half, precedence/edge-case items).

in_depth.label: Powers do not distribute over subtraction
in_depth.description: A power of a difference is not equal to the difference of powers; targets errors such as (a-b)^2 = a^2 - b^2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Close parallel to the addition case; similarly high distractor frequency in difference-of-squares contexts.

in_depth.label: Exponent precedence over multiplication
in_depth.description: Exponents are applied before multiplication unless grouping symbols change structure; focuses on scope of exponent inside a product term.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Order-of-operations precision required for correct evaluation of function expressions; moderately tested.

in_depth.label: Exponent precedence over addition
in_depth.description: Exponents are applied before addition or subtraction unless grouping symbols change structure; targets x^2 + 5 style expressions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Foundational order-of-operations skill; embedded in function evaluation tasks.

in_depth.label: Power of one
in_depth.description: Any base raised to the exponent 1 equals the base itself; tests recognition of a^1 = a.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trivial rule used incidentally; rarely the primary test object.

in_depth.label: One as a base
in_depth.description: The base 1 raised to any exponent remains 1; contrasts with exponent-of-1 rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Edge case; occasionally used to craft plausible distractors; low direct-test frequency.

umbrellas.label: Integer Exponent Basic Rules
umbrellas.description: Core rules for rewriting powers with integer exponents: product rule (add exponents for same-base products), quotient rule (subtract exponents for same-base quotients), and power of a power (multiply exponents).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.92 — product, quotient, and power-of-power rules are the most load-bearing exponent laws; ubiquitous in Units 1 and 2 and FRQ 4 symbolic manipulations. Split from integer_exponent_laws to satisfy 8-keyword limit.

in_depth.label: Product rule with same single-variable base
in_depth.description: Multiplying powers with the same variable base adds exponents while keeping the base; applies only when bases match exactly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core exponent law; appears in virtually every algebraic simplification involving powers.

in_depth.label: Product rule with same numeric base
in_depth.description: Multiplying powers with the same numeric base adds exponents; targets numeric powers such as 2^a · 2^b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Numeric-base product rule is tested in exponential function manipulation (2.4) and FRQ 4.

in_depth.label: Product rule with more than two same-base factors
in_depth.description: The product rule extends to three or more same-base powers by adding all exponents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Extended product chains appear in simplification problems; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Product rule requires matching bases
in_depth.description: Exponents are added only when multiplied powers share the same base; prevents combining unlike bases such as x^2·y^3.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Common error: students add exponents across unlike bases; this boundary is tested directly in MC.

in_depth.label: Quotient rule with same single-variable base
in_depth.description: Dividing powers with the same variable base subtracts the denominator exponent from the numerator exponent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core exponent law used in rational-function simplification and exponential manipulation.

in_depth.label: Quotient rule with same numeric base
in_depth.description: Dividing powers with the same numeric base subtracts exponents; targets powers of numbers such as 5^a/5^b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Numeric quotient rule appears in exponential function rewriting and semi-log contexts.

in_depth.label: Quotient rule producing a negative exponent
in_depth.description: When the denominator exponent exceeds the numerator, the quotient rule yields a negative exponent; connects quotient structure to reciprocal form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative-exponent results from quotient rule are high-frequency in simplification problems; connects to zero/negative exponent umbrella.

in_depth.label: Quotient rule producing a zero exponent
in_depth.description: Equal-exponent same-base quotients yield exponent zero; connects quotient structure to the zero-exponent rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero-exponent production via quotient rule is tested explicitly as a conceptual link.

in_depth.label: Power of a power rule
in_depth.description: A power raised to another power multiplies the exponents; targets (x^a)^b = x^(ab).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Power-of-power rule is one of the most-tested exponent laws; appears in exponential manipulation (2.4) and FRQ 4.

umbrellas.label: Integer Exponent Power-of-Product, Power-of-Quotient, and Same-Exponent Rules
umbrellas.description: Rules covering how exponents distribute over products and quotients: power of a product, power of a quotient, negative power on a quotient, and reverse direction (same-exponent regrouping).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Power-of-product and power-of-quotient rules are required for exponential function rewriting and polynomial manipulation; slightly lower than the product/quotient/power-of-power anchor but still high-load. Split from integer_exponent_laws to satisfy 8-keyword limit.

in_depth.label: Power of a power with negative exponents
in_depth.description: The power-of-power rule applies when one or both exponents are negative; resulting exponent is the product of the two exponents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative exponent interaction with power-of-power is a common source of sign errors in MC.

in_depth.label: Power of a power versus product rule
in_depth.description: Powers of powers multiply exponents; same-base products add exponents; this skill distinguishes these two commonly confused laws.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Confusion between these two rules is among the highest-frequency exponent errors; targeted heavily in both MC and FRQ 4.

in_depth.label: Power of a product rule
in_depth.description: A power applied to a product applies to each factor; (ab)^n = a^n · b^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Power-of-product rule is required for exponential function rewriting and polynomial manipulation.

in_depth.label: Power of a product with coefficients
in_depth.description: When a product with a numerical coefficient is raised to a power, both the coefficient and each variable factor are raised to that power; targets (3x)^2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Coefficient-in-base errors are high-frequency; directly tested in exponential manipulation problems.

in_depth.label: Power of a product with multiple variables
in_depth.description: When a product of several variable factors is raised to a power, the exponent applies to each factor; targets (xyz)^n style expressions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multi-variable power distribution appears in exponential expression manipulation.

in_depth.label: Power of a quotient rule
in_depth.description: A power applied to a quotient applies to both numerator and denominator; (a/b)^n = a^n/b^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Power-of-quotient structure appears in rational function and exponential expression work.

in_depth.label: Power of a quotient with negative exponents
in_depth.description: A quotient raised to a negative power is rewritten using reciprocal structure and positive exponents; targets (a/b)^(-n).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative power on quotient combines two laws; moderate complexity tested in simplification MC.

in_depth.label: Same exponent with different bases in a product
in_depth.description: A product of powers with the same exponent can be rewritten as one grouped product raised to that exponent; a^n · b^n = (ab)^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reverse direction of power-of-product rule; used in exponential expression equivalence questions.

in_depth.label: Same exponent with different bases in a quotient
in_depth.description: A quotient of powers with the same exponent can be rewritten as one quotient raised to that exponent; a^n/b^n = (a/b)^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reverse direction of power-of-quotient rule; used in equivalent-form questions.

umbrellas.label: Zero and Negative Exponent Basics
umbrellas.description: Meaning and rewriting of zero exponents (any nonzero base gives 1) and the core rules for negative exponents as reciprocals, including numerator/denominator factor movement and sign distinction.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.85 — negative exponents appear constantly in rational function and exponential work; required for FRQ 4 symbolic manipulations. Split from zero_and_negative_exponent_structure (first half).

in_depth.label: Zero exponent with nonzero base
in_depth.description: Any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals 1; the nonzero-base restriction is essential.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero-exponent rule is among the most tested exponent facts; frequently embedded in complex expressions.

in_depth.label: Variable-base restriction for zero exponents
in_depth.description: A variable base raised to zero equals 1 only when the base is nonzero; tests hidden restrictions in forms like x^0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain restriction from zero exponent is tested in AP Precalc function-domain contexts.

in_depth.label: Zero to the zero power
in_depth.description: The expression 0^0 is indeterminate or undefined in standard precalculus; distinguishes it from ordinary zero-exponent cases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Niche edge case; appears occasionally as a distractor option; low direct-test frequency.

in_depth.label: Negative exponent as reciprocal
in_depth.description: A negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the corresponding positive-exponent power; a^(-n) = 1/a^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Foundational negative-exponent rule; prerequisite for all rational function and exponential manipulation tasks.

in_depth.label: Negative exponent on denominator factor
in_depth.description: A factor with a negative exponent in the denominator moves to the numerator with a positive exponent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Denominator-to-numerator movement is heavily tested in simplification and equivalent-form questions.

in_depth.label: Negative exponent on numerator factor
in_depth.description: A factor with a negative exponent in the numerator moves to the denominator with a positive exponent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Numerator-to-denominator movement is the mirror of denominator movement; equally tested.

in_depth.label: Negative exponent versus negative number
in_depth.description: A negative exponent creates a reciprocal, not a negative value; 2^(-3) = 1/8, not -8.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-vs-reciprocal confusion is one of the highest-frequency exponent errors; consistently appears as a wrong-answer distractor.

umbrellas.label: Zero and Negative Exponent Advanced Forms
umbrellas.description: Advanced negative-exponent patterns: grouped-quantity bases, fractions raised to negative powers, positive-exponent convention, domain restrictions from negative exponents, and mixed-exponent simplification.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Advanced negative-exponent forms appear in FRQ 4 multi-step simplification; slightly lower yield than basic forms but still high-load. Split from zero_and_negative_exponent_structure (second half).

in_depth.label: Negative exponent on a grouped quantity
in_depth.description: A negative exponent on a grouped expression makes the entire grouped base reciprocal; differs from moving only one term inside the group.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Grouped-base scope errors with negative exponents produce common distractors.

in_depth.label: Negative exponent on a fraction
in_depth.description: A fraction raised to a negative exponent is rewritten by taking the reciprocal and applying the positive exponent; (a/b)^(-n) = (b/a)^n.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction raised to negative power combines reciprocal and exponent rules; tested in FRQ 4 equivalent-form tasks.

in_depth.label: Positive-exponent convention
in_depth.description: Algebraic answers are conventionally written without negative exponents by moving factors across a fraction bar; form convention only, not a change in value.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Positive-exponent form is expected in final answers on AP Precalc; tested as a completion step in simplification chains.

in_depth.label: Zero base with negative exponent
in_depth.description: A zero base raised to a negative exponent is undefined because it requires division by zero.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain restriction from negative exponent on potentially-zero base appears in function domain questions.

in_depth.label: Variable restrictions from negative exponents
in_depth.description: Negative exponents create denominator factors in reciprocal form; any base moved to the denominator cannot be zero.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain-restriction identification from negative exponents is tested in AP Precalc function-domain contexts.

in_depth.label: Mixed positive, zero, and negative exponents
in_depth.description: Expressions may contain positive, zero, and negative exponents simultaneously; each type must be interpreted correctly in the same expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Mixed-exponent simplification problems are a staple of AP Precalc MC symbolic fluency questions.

in_depth.label: Negative exponents produced by quotient rule
in_depth.description: Applying the quotient rule may produce negative exponents; these can then be rewritten as reciprocal factors.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-step chain (quotient rule then reciprocal rewrite) is a canonical simplification task in exponent manipulation.

umbrellas.label: Scientific Notation with Powers of Ten
umbrellas.description: Operations and comparisons involving scientific notation using exponent laws and powers of ten, including multiplication, division, addition, subtraction, coefficient normalization, and magnitude comparison.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.30 — minimal direct AP Precalc overlap; powers of 10 appear in semi-log contexts (2.15) but scientific notation itself is not a central exam skill.

in_depth.label: Multiplication in scientific notation
in_depth.description: Numbers in scientific notation are multiplied by multiplying coefficients and adding powers of 10; coefficient may need normalization.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Most directly connected to exponent-law practice; occasionally appears in exponential-context problems.

in_depth.label: Division in scientific notation
in_depth.description: Numbers in scientific notation are divided by dividing coefficients and subtracting powers of 10; result may need coefficient adjustment.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Quotient-rule application in scientific-notation context; low AP exam overlap.

in_depth.label: Addition in scientific notation with same power
in_depth.description: Scientific-notation values with the same power of 10 are added by combining coefficients while keeping the common power.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Procedural skill; minimal AP Precalc exam appearance.

in_depth.label: Addition in scientific notation with different powers
in_depth.description: Scientific-notation values with different powers of 10 must be rewritten with a common power before coefficients can be combined.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rarely tested at AP Precalc level; prerequisite alignment skill.

in_depth.label: Subtraction in scientific notation
in_depth.description: Scientific-notation subtraction requires matching powers of 10 before subtracting coefficients; targets sign and place-value issues.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel to addition with different powers; same low AP exam frequency.

in_depth.label: Scientific-notation coefficient adjustment
in_depth.description: After an operation, the coefficient must satisfy 1 ≤ |a| < 10, with the power of 10 adjusted accordingly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Normalization step needed after any operation; supports fluency but not a direct exam target.

in_depth.label: Scientific-notation comparison
in_depth.description: Scientific-notation values are compared using powers of 10 first and coefficients when powers match.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Magnitude comparison skill; occasionally tested in data-interpretation contexts.

in_depth.label: Scientific notation with negative powers of ten
in_depth.description: Negative powers of 10 represent small decimal quantities; tests operations and interpretation for values less than 1.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative-power-of-ten context overlaps with semi-log reasoning (2.15); slightly higher yield than non-negative cases.

umbrellas.label: Rational Exponent Meaning and Conversion
umbrellas.description: Meaning of fractional exponents: unit fractions as roots, m/n form (denominator = root index, numerator = power), equivalence with radical notation, and order of root vs power operations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.88 — rational exponents are explicitly part of AP Precalc 2.3 and 2.4 and appear in FRQ 4 equivalent-form manipulations. Split from rational_exponent_structure (first half, meaning/conversion skills).

in_depth.label: Unit fraction exponents as roots
in_depth.description: An exponent of 1/n represents an nth root; connects x^(1/2) to square roots and x^(1/3) to cube roots.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core rational-exponent concept directly tested in 2.3 and 2.4; high FRQ 4 relevance.

in_depth.label: Denominator of rational exponent as root index
in_depth.description: In a rational exponent m/n, the denominator n gives the root index; focuses on interpreting the root component.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Denominator-as-root-index is foundational for converting between radical and rational-exponent forms.

in_depth.label: Numerator of rational exponent as power
in_depth.description: In a rational exponent m/n, the numerator m gives the power applied to the base or its root.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Numerator-as-power is the complementary half of rational-exponent meaning; equally tested.

in_depth.label: Root-then-power versus power-then-root
in_depth.description: For real-valued expressions, x^(m/n) can be interpreted as nth root then mth power, or mth power then nth root; focuses on equivalent rational-exponent structures.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Order flexibility is tested when one interpretation produces simpler computation; AP Precalc MC regular.

in_depth.label: Negative rational exponents
in_depth.description: A negative rational exponent combines reciprocal structure with root and power; x^(-m/n) = 1 / (nth root of x^m).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative rational exponents appear in exponential function manipulation and FRQ 4; combine multiple rules.

in_depth.label: Rational exponents on perfect powers
in_depth.description: Rational exponents often yield integer results when the base is a perfect power matching the root index; e.g., 27^(2/3) = 9.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Evaluating rational exponents on perfect powers is a staple no-calculator MC question.

in_depth.label: Even-denominator rational exponents and real domain
in_depth.description: Rational exponents with even denominators involve even roots; the base must be nonnegative for real-valued results.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain restriction from even-root rational exponents appears in function domain analysis.

umbrellas.label: Rational Exponent Laws and Domain
umbrellas.description: Exponent laws extended to rational exponents (product, quotient, power-of-power), domain restrictions from even/odd denominators, lowest-terms form, and variable restrictions.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Rational-exponent law application and domain analysis feed FRQ 4 multi-step manipulation tasks; slightly lower yield than meaning/conversion. Split from rational_exponent_structure (second half, law/domain skills).

in_depth.label: Product rule with rational exponents
in_depth.description: Same-base powers with rational exponents are multiplied by adding the rational exponents; extends integer product rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rational-exponent product rule is required in exponential expression simplification tasks.

in_depth.label: Quotient rule with rational exponents
in_depth.description: Same-base powers with rational exponents are divided by subtracting the denominator rational exponent from the numerator.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rational-exponent quotient rule parallels integer form; used in exponential function rewriting.

in_depth.label: Power of a power with rational exponents
in_depth.description: When a power with a rational exponent is raised to another power, the exponents are multiplied; (x^(m/n))^p = x^(mp/n).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Power-of-power with rational exponents is a canonical FRQ 4 manipulation step.

in_depth.label: Odd-denominator rational exponents with negative bases
in_depth.description: Rational exponents with odd denominators can represent real odd roots of negative bases when written in reduced form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Odd-root negative-base behavior distinguishes cube roots from square roots; moderately tested.

in_depth.label: Rational exponents in lowest terms
in_depth.description: Real interpretation of rational exponents can depend on the exponent being in lowest terms, especially for negative bases; e.g., (-1)^(2/4) vs (-1)^(1/2).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Lowest-terms requirement is a subtle precision issue; tested occasionally in error-analysis MC.

in_depth.label: Variable restrictions from rational exponents
in_depth.description: Variable expressions with rational exponents may require nonnegative or nonzero bases depending on even roots and negative exponents.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain-restriction identification from rational exponents appears in AP Precalc function-domain problems.

umbrellas.label: Radical Notation and Root Basics
umbrellas.description: Foundational radical reading: radicand and index identification, square/cube/nth-root structure, principal square root, and the behavior of even roots (nonnegativity requirement, nonreal negatives).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Radical structure is prerequisite for simplifying exact trig values (3.3), complex zeros (1.5), and all radical operation and equation work; moderately tested directly. Split from radical_notation_and_root_structure (first half, structure/even-root items).

in_depth.label: Radicand identification
in_depth.description: The radicand is the expression inside a radical symbol; distinguishes radicand from index or outside coefficient.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural reading skill; foundational but not a direct test target.

in_depth.label: Root index identification
in_depth.description: The index of a radical indicates which root is taken; a square root has implied index 2.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Index identification is prerequisite for all root-type distinctions; foundational.

in_depth.label: Square root structure
in_depth.description: A square root asks for the principal value whose square equals the radicand; foundational meaning before simplification.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Square-root meaning is the entry point for radical work; tested implicitly throughout.

in_depth.label: Cube root structure
in_depth.description: A cube root asks for the value whose cube equals the radicand; cube roots can produce real values for negative radicands.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cube root behavior with negatives distinguishes it from square roots; tested in exact-value and domain questions.

in_depth.label: Nth-root structure
in_depth.description: An nth root asks for a value that raised to the nth power gives the radicand; generalizes square and cube root structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: General nth-root understanding supports rational exponent interpretation.

in_depth.label: Principal square root
in_depth.description: The radical symbol √x represents the nonnegative principal square root; distinguishes √9 = 3 from the two solutions of x^2 = 9.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Principal-root vs ±root confusion is a high-frequency error in both equation solving and function domain work.

in_depth.label: Even roots of negative numbers are nonreal
in_depth.description: Even roots of negative numbers are not real in standard precalculus; drives domain restrictions for square roots and other even roots.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Even-root domain restriction is tested directly in AP Precalc function-domain problems and implicit in 1.5 (complex zeros).

umbrellas.label: Radical Notation and Root Domain
umbrellas.description: Domain and sign behavior of radicals: even-root nonnegativity conditions, odd-root sign behavior, coefficient outside a radical, and nested radical structure.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Domain and sign distinctions for radicals are tested in function-domain and error-analysis problems; slightly lower direct yield than structural identification. Split from radical_notation_and_root_structure (second half, domain/behavior items).

in_depth.label: Even roots of positive numbers
in_depth.description: Even roots of positive numbers have positive principal values in radical notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Baseline behavior for even roots; foundational for domain reasoning.

in_depth.label: Even roots of zero
in_depth.description: Any even root of zero equals zero; a defined boundary case for even-root expressions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Boundary edge case; seldom the primary question target.

in_depth.label: Odd roots of negative numbers
in_depth.description: Odd roots of negative numbers are real and negative; distinguishes cube roots and fifth roots from square roots.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Odd-root negative behavior is tested in exact-value computation and domain analysis.

in_depth.label: Even-root radicand condition
in_depth.description: For real-valued even-root expressions the radicand must be ≥ 0; applies to square roots, fourth roots, and all even-index radicals.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain condition from even roots is one of the most tested foundation skills in AP Precalc function analysis.

in_depth.label: Odd-root radicands allow all real values
in_depth.description: Odd-root expressions are real for positive, zero, and negative radicands; no nonnegativity restriction.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Contrasting with even-root restriction; tested when students must identify unrestricted domains.

in_depth.label: Coefficient outside a radical
in_depth.description: A number or expression outside a radical multiplies the radical expression; distinguishes 3√x from √(3x).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Scope of coefficient vs radicand is tested in simplification and combining-like-radicals problems.

in_depth.label: Nested radical structure
in_depth.description: A nested radical contains one radical expression inside another; tests recognition of layered root structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Nested radicals appear in advanced simplification; less frequent at AP Precalc level.

umbrellas.label: Radical Simplification — Numeric Factors
umbrellas.description: Using perfect powers, prime factorization, and radical product/quotient properties to simplify radical expressions with numeric radicands; includes combining into a single radical and index-matching rules.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.78 — simplifying radicals required for exact trig values (3.3), complex zero work (1.5), and FRQ 4 symbolic tasks. Split from radical_factor_structure_and_rewriting (first half, numeric items).

in_depth.label: Perfect-square factors in square roots
in_depth.description: Square-root radicands are analyzed for perfect-square factors; those factors are pulled outside the radical.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Extracting perfect-square factors is the most common radical-simplification step; foundational for exact trig values.

in_depth.label: Perfect-cube factors in cube roots
in_depth.description: Cube-root radicands are analyzed for perfect-cube factors, which are pulled outside the radical.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cube-root simplification appears in exact-value and equivalent-form tasks.

in_depth.label: Perfect nth-power factors
in_depth.description: An nth-root radicand is analyzed for factors that are perfect nth powers; generalizes square-root and cube-root factor extraction.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Generalized nth-power extraction supports rational-exponent equivalence; moderate AP frequency.

in_depth.label: Prime factorization in radicals
in_depth.description: Prime factorization reveals groups of factors matching the radical index when perfect-power factors are not obvious.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Prime factorization method is a reliable fallback strategy tested when perfect powers are not immediately visible.

in_depth.label: Radical product property
in_depth.description: For real-valued radicals with appropriate conditions, a root of a product equals the product of roots; supports splitting radicands.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radical product property is the mechanism for all factor-extraction simplification; very high usage.

in_depth.label: Radical quotient property
in_depth.description: A root of a quotient equals the quotient of roots for real-valued radicals with appropriate conditions; separates numerator and denominator radicands.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radical quotient property used in rationalizing and equivalent-form tasks.

in_depth.label: Combining factors into a single radical
in_depth.description: Products or quotients of same-index radicals can be combined into one radical; reverse of product and quotient properties.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reverse-direction combination appears in rationalization setup and simplification verification.

in_depth.label: Matching radical indices for combining
in_depth.description: Radicals can be combined by product or quotient properties only when root indices match; prevents combining square roots with cube roots.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Index-matching requirement is a boundary tested in error-analysis problems.

umbrellas.label: Radical Simplification — Variable Factors
umbrellas.description: Simplifying radical expressions involving variable powers: extracting even and odd power factors, absolute value requirements, general nth-root exponent division, lowest-terms form, and combined numeric-variable cases.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Variable-power radical simplification (including absolute value) is a high-frequency AP Precalc MC task; slightly higher yield than purely numeric simplification due to domain interaction. Split from radical_factor_structure_and_rewriting (second half, variable items).

in_depth.label: Square root of a variable even power
in_depth.description: The square root of a variable raised to an even power is rewritten using half the exponent; absolute value is needed when variables are unrestricted.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variable even-power extraction is a canonical AP Precalc radical rewriting task; absolute value requirement is frequently tested.

in_depth.label: Square root of a variable odd power
in_depth.description: A square root of an odd variable power separates into an outside variable power and one remaining variable factor inside; e.g., √(x^5) = x^2·√x.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Odd-power extraction is a standard radical simplification step in AP Precalc symbolic manipulation.

in_depth.label: Nth root of variable powers by exponent division
in_depth.description: For an nth root of a variable power, the exponent is divided by the root index using quotient and remainder to determine outside and leftover factors.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Exponent-division reasoning is required for general radical simplification with variable radicands.

in_depth.label: Absolute value from even roots
in_depth.description: Even roots of even powers require absolute value to ensure the principal root is nonnegative; √(x^2) = |x|.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Absolute-value requirement from even roots is a high-frequency AP Precalc question; commonly missed.

in_depth.label: Odd roots of variable powers
in_depth.description: Odd roots of variable powers do not require absolute value because odd roots preserve sign; ∛(x^3) = x.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Contrasting with even-root absolute value; tested in simplification and domain problems.

in_depth.label: Radical in lowest terms
in_depth.description: A radical is fully simplified when no perfect-power factor matching the index remains inside, no avoidable fraction remains inside, and no radical remains in the denominator.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Simplified radical form is the expected answer convention on AP Precalc; tested as a final-answer requirement.

in_depth.label: Radical rewriting with numeric and variable factors
in_depth.description: Radical expressions with both numerical perfect-power factors and variable-power factors require extracting both types simultaneously; e.g., √(72x^5).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Combined numeric-and-variable simplification is a standard AP Precalc MC task.

in_depth.label: Radicals with existing outside coefficients
in_depth.description: When a radical already has an outside coefficient, extracted factors multiply that coefficient; targets forms such as 3√50.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Coefficient multiplication after extraction is tested as a multi-step simplification.

umbrellas.label: Radical Addition and Subtraction
umbrellas.description: Identifying like radicals (same index and radicand), adding and subtracting like radical terms by combining outside coefficients, and common misconceptions about combining radicands directly.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: 0.72 — combining and subtracting radicals appear in trig identity/equation solving (FRQ 4) and exact trig value computation (3.3). Split from radical_operations_and_like_radicals (first half, addition/subtraction items).

in_depth.label: Like radicals with same index and radicand
in_depth.description: Like radicals share the same root index and the same radicand after full simplification; only their outside coefficients differ.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Identifying like radicals is the prerequisite step for all radical addition and subtraction.

in_depth.label: Unlike radicals with different radicands
in_depth.description: Radicals with different radicands are unlike and cannot be combined unless they simplify to the same radicand.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Unlike-radicand boundary is tested in error-analysis items; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Unlike radicals with different indices
in_depth.description: Radicals with different root indices are unlike even when their radicands match; prevents combining √5 and ∛5.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Index-mismatch error is tested in error-analysis and classification questions.

in_depth.label: Adding like square roots
in_depth.description: Like square-root terms are added by combining outside coefficients while keeping the radical unchanged.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct like-radical addition is the most common radical operation in AP Precalc simplification tasks.

in_depth.label: Subtracting like square roots
in_depth.description: Like square-root terms are subtracted by subtracting outside coefficients while keeping the radical unchanged.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel to addition; equally tested in simplification problems.

in_depth.label: Adding and subtracting like nth roots
in_depth.description: Like radicals of any matching index can be combined by adding or subtracting outside coefficients; index and radicand stay unchanged.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Generalization beyond square roots; moderately tested at AP Precalc level.

in_depth.label: Combining radicals after rewriting
in_depth.description: Radicals that look unlike may become like after perfect-power factors are extracted; targets forms such as √8 + √18.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Simplify-then-combine is a canonical two-step AP Precalc MC task; high frequency.

in_depth.label: Radical addition does not combine radicands
in_depth.description: A sum of radicals is not found by adding radicands; targets the misconception √a + √b = √(a+b).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radicand-addition error mirrors the power-distribution error; one of the highest-frequency radical misconceptions in MC distractors.

in_depth.label: Radical subtraction does not combine radicands
in_depth.description: A difference of radicals is not found by subtracting radicands; targets the misconception √a - √b = √(a-b).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel misconception to addition case; similarly tested as a distractor.

umbrellas.label: Radical Multiplication and Binomial Products
umbrellas.description: Multiplying and dividing radicals, FOIL-style distribution with radical binomials, squaring binomial radicals, and conjugate products that eliminate radical terms.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Radical multiplication and conjugate products are directly needed for rationalizing denominators and trig identity work; slightly higher practical yield than like-radical addition. Split from radical_operations_and_like_radicals (second half, multiplication/binomial items).

in_depth.label: Multiplying square roots
in_depth.description: Square roots are multiplied by multiplying radicands, with outside coefficients multiplied separately.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radical multiplication used in rationalization and exact trig value computation.

in_depth.label: Multiplying nth roots with the same index
in_depth.description: Radicals with the same index can be multiplied by multiplying their radicands; generalizes beyond square roots.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Generalized radical multiplication supports rational-exponent equivalence work.

in_depth.label: Dividing radicals with the same index
in_depth.description: Radicals with the same index can be divided by forming a radical quotient; uses the radical quotient property.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radical division is the setup step for simplification and rationalizing tasks.

in_depth.label: Multiplying binomial radical expressions
in_depth.description: Products of binomials containing radicals require distribution, with resulting radical products simplified; (a + √b)(c + √d).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Binomial radical multiplication is required in rationalizing conjugate denominators and trig identity proofs.

in_depth.label: Squaring binomial radical expressions
in_depth.description: A binomial containing a radical must be squared using full binomial multiplication; (a + √b)^2 ≠ a^2 + b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Squaring with radicals appears in equation-solving and rationalization; missing middle term is a common error.

in_depth.label: Conjugate products with radicals
in_depth.description: A product of radical conjugates creates a difference of squares that eliminates the radical cross-terms; (a + √b)(a - √b) = a^2 - b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conjugate product is the mechanism for rationalizing binomial denominators; directly tested in FRQ 4 equivalent-form tasks.

umbrellas.label: Rationalizing Monomial Radical Denominators
umbrellas.description: Removing radicals from monomial denominators: single square-root, coefficient-bearing, nth-root, and variable radical denominators.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Rationalizing appears in FRQ 4 equivalent-form manipulation and exact trig value computation; moderately tested directly but frequently an intermediate step. Split from rationalizing_and_radical_denominators (first half, monomial cases).

in_depth.label: Radical denominator convention
in_depth.description: Many algebraic forms are conventionally rewritten so radicals do not remain in denominators; establishes the goal before techniques are applied.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Contextual motivation for rationalizing; foundational awareness skill.

in_depth.label: Single square-root denominator
in_depth.description: A denominator containing one square root is rationalized by multiplying numerator and denominator by the same square root.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Monomial radical-denominator rationalization is the most common rationalizing task; tested frequently.

in_depth.label: Square-root denominator with coefficient
in_depth.description: A denominator of the form a√b is rationalized by multiplying by √b while tracking the numerical coefficient.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Coefficient tracking in rationalization is tested as a procedural precision item.

in_depth.label: Nth-root denominator
in_depth.description: An nth-root denominator is rationalized by multiplying by a radical factor that completes a perfect nth power; generalizes square-root rationalization.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Nth-root rationalization connects to rational-exponent equivalence; moderately tested.

in_depth.label: Variable radical denominator
in_depth.description: Radical denominators containing variables require multiplying by factors that remove the radical while respecting variable restrictions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variable radical denominators appear in algebraic manipulation tasks and function expression rewriting.

umbrellas.label: Rationalizing Binomial Denominators and Numerators
umbrellas.description: Removing radicals from binomial denominators using conjugates, rationalizing numerators, and preserving domain restrictions through the rewriting process.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Conjugate rationalization of binomial denominators is directly tested in FRQ 4; slightly higher yield than monomial rationalization due to more complex algebraic manipulation required. Split from rationalizing_and_radical_denominators (second half, binomial/conjugate cases).

in_depth.label: Binomial square-root denominator
in_depth.description: A denominator containing a binomial with a square root is rationalized by multiplying by its conjugate, removing the radical via difference of squares.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conjugate rationalization of binomial denominators is a standard FRQ 4 symbolic manipulation.

in_depth.label: Conjugate selection for rationalizing
in_depth.description: The conjugate of a binomial changes the sign between terms while keeping the terms unchanged; it is the key multiplier for binomial radical denominators.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Correct conjugate selection is a procedural skill tested in rationalization problems.

in_depth.label: Denominator with two radical terms
in_depth.description: A denominator containing two square-root terms is rationalized by multiplying by the conjugate; targets 1/(√a + √b).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-radical denominator rationalization is tested in equivalent-form MC and FRQ 4 tasks.

in_depth.label: Rationalizing a numerator
in_depth.description: A radical numerator can be rationalized using conjugate or radical-factor logic; particularly relevant for limit-style equivalent-form work.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Numerator rationalization appears in calculus preparation contexts and occasional AP Precalc equivalent-form tasks.

in_depth.label: Restrictions when rationalizing
in_depth.description: Rationalizing preserves value only when multiplied factors are defined and nonzero; restrictions must not be lost during rewriting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain-restriction preservation during rationalization is tested in AP Precalc function analysis.

umbrellas.label: Radical Equations — Isolating and Solving
umbrellas.description: Isolating radicals and solving radical equations by raising both sides to an appropriate power: square-root equations with linear or quadratic results, equations requiring two squaring steps, and cube/nth-root equations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Radical equation solving has direct F6 chapter-test and AP Precalc exam presence; rescored from 0.60 to 0.65 per audit finding that its direct link to chapter test items was underweighted. Split from radical_equations_and_rational_exponent_equations (first half, isolation/solving items).

in_depth.label: Radical equation structure
in_depth.description: A radical equation contains a variable inside a radical expression; distinguishes radical equations from equations with radical constants only.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Classification skill; foundational for solving strategy selection.

in_depth.label: Isolated square-root equations
in_depth.description: A square-root equation with one isolated radical is solved by squaring both sides; √(ax + b) = c structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Basic isolated radical equation is the entry-level radical-solving problem; moderate AP frequency.

in_depth.label: Non-isolated square-root equations
in_depth.description: When a radical is not isolated, it must be isolated before both sides are squared; targets equations like √(x+1) + 3 = 8.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Isolation-before-squaring is a key procedural step; tested for correct order-of-operations in equation solving.

in_depth.label: Square-root equations with linear radicands
in_depth.description: A square-root equation with a linear expression inside the radicand produces a linear equation after squaring; √(ax + b) = c.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Linear-radicand equations are the most common radical equation form in AP Precalc MC.

in_depth.label: Square-root equations leading to quadratics
in_depth.description: Some square-root equations produce quadratic equations after squaring when a variable expression appears outside the radical; requires checking for extraneous solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Radical-to-quadratic equations test both radical and quadratic solving skills together; moderate AP frequency with extraneous-solution check required.

in_depth.label: Radical equations with radicals on both sides
in_depth.description: When radicals appear on both sides of an equation, squaring may remove both radicals or require a second squaring step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-sided radical equations are more complex; appear occasionally in AP Precalc MC.

in_depth.label: Radical equations requiring two squaring steps
in_depth.description: Equations with multiple radicals require isolating one radical, squaring, then isolating and squaring again.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-squaring equations are procedurally complex; less common on AP Precalc but tests multi-step fluency.

umbrellas.label: Radical Equations — Extraneous Solutions and Rational-Exponent Equations
umbrellas.description: Cube and nth-root equations, rational-exponent equations solved via reciprocal powers, extraneous-solution checking, domain restrictions in equation contexts, and no-solution recognition.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Extraneous-solution checking and rational-exponent equations are high-discrimination exam items; yield matches the umbrella-level rescore to 0.65. Split from radical_equations_and_rational_exponent_equations (second half).

in_depth.label: Cube-root equations
in_depth.description: A cube-root equation is solved by cubing both sides after isolating the cube-root expression; allows negative radicands.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cube-root equations appear in AP Precalc alongside other radical equation types.

in_depth.label: Nth-root equations
in_depth.description: An nth-root equation is solved by raising both sides to the nth power after isolating the radical; even-index roots require attention to real-valued restrictions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: General nth-root equation solving appears occasionally; supports rational-exponent equation mastery.

in_depth.label: Rational-exponent equations
in_depth.description: Equations involving rational exponents are solved by raising both sides to the reciprocal power or rewriting in radical form; x^(m/n) = c structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rational-exponent equations appear in AP Precalc as applications of 2.4 and directly in FRQ 4.

in_depth.label: Reciprocal powers in rational-exponent equations
in_depth.description: To undo a rational exponent m/n, both sides are raised to the reciprocal power n/m, with domain and extraneous-solution checks.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reciprocal-power technique is directly tested in FRQ 4 and connects rational exponent manipulation to equation solving.

in_depth.label: Extraneous solutions from squaring
in_depth.description: Squaring both sides of a radical equation can introduce solutions that fail the original equation; every candidate solution must be checked.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Extraneous solution checking is a high-yield exam skill; one of the most tested concepts in radical equations.

in_depth.label: Domain restrictions in radical equations
in_depth.description: Radical equations with even-index roots require nonnegative radicands and matching principal-root behavior; these restrictions guide valid solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain restriction reasoning in equation contexts is tested in AP Precalc and directly connects to function domain analysis.

in_depth.label: Radical equations with no real solution
in_depth.description: A radical equation may have no real solution because of domain restrictions, principal-root restrictions, or all candidates being extraneous after solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-solution recognition is a key exam discriminator tested in AP Precalc equation-solving problems.
